note_data.title: Sets and functions
keyPoints: A **set** is a collection of well-defined objects called elements
Use **set-builder notation** {x : condition} or **list notation** {a, b, c} to describe sets
The **universal set** ξ contains all elements under consideration in a particular context
**Venn diagrams** use overlapping circles to show relationships between sets with the universal set as a rectangle
A **function** is a mapping where each element in the domain maps to exactly one element in the range
The **inverse function** f⁻¹(x) reverses the effect of function f(x), so f⁻¹(f(x)) = x
A **composite function** (g ∘ f)(x) = g(f(x)) means apply f first, then apply g to the result
**Domain** is the set of input values, **range** is the set of possible output values

definitions.definition: A mapping from a set of inputs (domain) to a set of outputs (range) where each input has exactly one output
A function f⁻¹ that reverses the mapping of function f, where f⁻¹(f(x)) = x for all x in the domain
The combination of two functions where the output of the first function becomes the input of the second function
The set containing all elements under consideration in a particular problem

howToAnswer.instruction: Write elements separated by commas inside curly brackets { }
For composite functions, substitute the inner function into the outer function step by step
For Venn diagrams, draw circles inside a rectangle, label all regions clearly

modelAnswer.question: Given f(x) = 2x + 3 and g(x) = x², find (a) f⁻¹(x) (b) (g ∘ f)(5)
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct method for finding inverse

commonMistakes: Writing f⁻¹(x) = 1/f(x) instead of finding the actual inverse function
Confusing (f ∘ g)(x) with (g ∘ f)(x) - the order matters in composite functions
Not showing all elements clearly in Venn diagram regions
Forgetting to state domain restrictions when finding inverse functions
Writing ∈ instead of ⊆ when describing subset relationships
examinerTips: Functions appear in Paper 2 almost every year, often worth 8-12 marks
Always check your inverse function by verifying f(f⁻¹(x)) = x
For Venn diagrams, shade the required region clearly and label with numbers
Show working step-by-step for composite functions to earn method marks
formulas: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
If f(x) = ax + b, then f⁻¹(x) = (x-b)/a
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Venn diagram showing two overlapping circles A and B inside a rectangle (universal set ξ), with regions labeled: A only, B only, A∩B (intersection), and elements outside both circles
